title: Trained immunity modulators: A new frontier in immunoregulation and disease intervention
research_question: How do trained immunity modulators (inducers and suppressors) regulate innate immune memory, and what are their therapeutic potentials and limitations in immune-related diseases?
methods_summary: Review of molecular mechanisms underlying trained immunity, including metabolic and epigenetic reprogramming
Classification and mechanistic analysis of trained immunity modulators (vaccines, polysaccharides, nanobiologics, endogenous mediators, etc.)
Evaluation of dual roles of trained immunity in infectious diseases, chronic inflammatory disorders, and cancer
key_findings: Trained immunity modulators include inducers (e.g., BCG, MMR, β-glucan, LPS) and suppressors, which reprogram innate immune cells via metabolic and epigenetic changes
Trained immunity provides heterologous protection against infections but can exacerbate chronic inflammatory diseases and cancer progression
Combining trained immunity inducers with immunotherapies (e.g., checkpoint inhibitors) shows promise for enhanced anti-tumor efficacy
extracted_insights: Trained immunity represents a paradigm shift in immunology, where innate immune cells acquire memory-like properties through metabolic and epigenetic reprogramming. This review comprehensively categorizes modulators (inducers and suppressors) and highlights their dual roles: beneficial in infection control and cancer therapy, but detrimental in chronic inflammation. Key modulators like BCG and β-glucan show clinical potential, especially when combined with adaptive immunotherapies, though challenges remain in balancing efficacy and safety.
